key_facts: Respondent George Kayer murdered Delbert Haas in 1994
Kayer devised a plan to rob Haas and shot him pointblank in the head
Kayer stole Haas' wallet, watch, and jewelry, and later returned to shoot Haas again
Kayer was found guilty of premeditated first-degree murder and related offenses
Kayer refused to cooperate with a mitigation specialist during sentencing

Arizona law required a judge to determine whether aggravating circumstances were established, and a judge could impose a sentence of death only if at least one such circumstance was shown and there was no mitigating circumstance that was sufficient to call for leniency
Kayer argued ineffective assistance of counsel because his attorneys failed to investigate mitigating circumstances
The sentencing judge held a 9-day evidentiary hearing and denied relief
The Ninth Circuit panel reversed, finding that Kayer's mitigation evidence likely affected his sentence
Arizona sought and was denied rehearing en banc by the Ninth Circuit
legal_issues: Whether Kayer's death sentence was imposed in violation of his Sixth Amendment right to effective assistance of counsel
Whether the Ninth Circuit erred in ordering issuance of a writ of habeas corpus despite ample room for reasonable disagreement about the prisoner's ineffective-assistance-of-counsel claim
decision: The Court of Appeals erred in ordering issuance of a writ of habeas corpus despite ample room for reasonable disagreement about the prisoner's ineffective-assistance-of-counsel claim. The Court therefore granted the petition for certiorari and vacated the judgment below.
reasoning_summary: The Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996 (AEDPA) restricts the power of federal courts to grant writs of habeas corpus based on claims that were 'adjudicated on the merits' by a state court. When a state court has applied clearly established federal law to reasonably determined facts in the process of adjudicating a claim on the merits, a federal habeas court may not disturb the state court's decision unless its error lies 'beyond any possibility for fairminded disagreement.' The Ninth Circuit clearly violated this Court's AEDPA jurisprudence by ordering issuance of a writ of habeas corpus despite ample room for reasonable disagreement about the prisoner's ineffective-assistance-of-counsel claim.
